verses.text: Jacob overhears Laban's sons grumbling — Jacob has taken away all that was our father's. And Laban's face toward him is no longer kind. The peace between them is over.
Jacob overhears Laban's sons grumbling — Jacob has taken away all that was our father's. And Laban's face toward him is no longer kind. The peace between them is over.
The LORD speaks: return to the land of your fathers and your kindred; I will be with thee. The same promise from Bethel meets him at the end of his exile.

Jacob calls Rachel and Leah out to the field — away from listening ears — and tells them everything. Your father has changed my wages ten times. But God did not let him hurt me. The visible deceit has been met by invisible protection.

Jacob calls Rachel and Leah out to the field — away from listening ears — and tells them everything. Your father has changed my wages ten times. But God did not let him hurt me. The visible deceit has been met by invisible protection.

Jacob calls Rachel and Leah out to the field — away from listening ears — and tells them everything. Your father has changed my wages ten times. But God did not let him hurt me. The visible deceit has been met by invisible protection.

Jacob calls Rachel and Leah out to the field — away from listening ears — and tells them everything. Your father has changed my wages ten times. But God did not let him hurt me. The visible deceit has been met by invisible protection.

If he said the speckled were my wages, the flock bore speckled. If he said the streaked, they bore streaked. God has taken the cattle from your father and given them to me. Genesis credits God, not Jacob's branches.

If he said the speckled were my wages, the flock bore speckled. If he said the streaked, they bore streaked. God has taken the cattle from your father and given them to me. Genesis credits God, not Jacob's branches.

Then Jacob tells of a dream: an angel of God spoke to him. I have seen all that Laban does to you. I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed the pillar and vowed a vow. Now arise, get thee out of this land, and return. The dream sends him home.

Then Jacob tells of a dream: an angel of God spoke to him. I have seen all that Laban does to you. I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed the pillar and vowed a vow. Now arise, get thee out of this land, and return. The dream sends him home.

Then Jacob tells of a dream: an angel of God spoke to him. I have seen all that Laban does to you. I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed the pillar and vowed a vow. Now arise, get thee out of this land, and return. The dream sends him home.

Then Jacob tells of a dream: an angel of God spoke to him. I have seen all that Laban does to you. I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed the pillar and vowed a vow. Now arise, get thee out of this land, and return. The dream sends him home.

Rachel and Leah answer with one voice for the first time in the story: is there any inheritance left for us in our father's house? He has sold us and consumed our price. Whatever God has said to you, do it. The sisters who fought for sons agree on this.

Rachel and Leah answer with one voice for the first time in the story: is there any inheritance left for us in our father's house? He has sold us and consumed our price. Whatever God has said to you, do it. The sisters who fought for sons agree on this.

Rachel and Leah answer with one voice for the first time in the story: is there any inheritance left for us in our father's house? He has sold us and consumed our price. Whatever God has said to you, do it. The sisters who fought for sons agree on this.
Jacob sets his sons and wives on camels, takes all his livestock and possessions, and starts the long walk back to Canaan. Twenty years of accumulation moving in a single train.
Jacob sets his sons and wives on camels, takes all his livestock and possessions, and starts the long walk back to Canaan. Twenty years of accumulation moving in a single train.
Laban has gone to shear his sheep. While he is away, Rachel steals her father's household idols — the teraphim. Even the favoured wife is still attached to objects she shouldn't be.
Jacob steals away unawares — without a word — and crosses the Euphrates, his face set toward Mount Gilead. The man known for leaving in secret leaves once more. Old patterns travel with him.
Jacob steals away unawares — without a word — and crosses the Euphrates, his face set toward Mount Gilead. The man known for leaving in secret leaves once more. Old patterns travel with him.

Three days later Laban hears, gathers his men, pursues for seven days, and overtakes Jacob in the hill country of Gilead. That night God comes to Laban in a dream: take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad. God restrains pursuit without removing it.

Three days later Laban hears, gathers his men, pursues for seven days, and overtakes Jacob in the hill country of Gilead. That night God comes to Laban in a dream: take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad. God restrains pursuit without removing it.

Three days later Laban hears, gathers his men, pursues for seven days, and overtakes Jacob in the hill country of Gilead. That night God comes to Laban in a dream: take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad. God restrains pursuit without removing it.
Laban catches up and confronts him: what have you done? You have carried away my daughters like captives. I would have sent you off with music and kisses. You did this foolishly.
Laban catches up and confronts him: what have you done? You have carried away my daughters like captives. I would have sent you off with music and kisses. You did this foolishly.
Laban catches up and confronts him: what have you done? You have carried away my daughters like captives. I would have sent you off with music and kisses. You did this foolishly.

Laban continues: you did not even let me kiss my children goodbye. I could have harmed you, but the God of your father warned me last night. And then a smaller, sharper question: but why did you steal my gods?

Laban continues: you did not even let me kiss my children goodbye. I could have harmed you, but the God of your father warned me last night. And then a smaller, sharper question: but why did you steal my gods?

Laban continues: you did not even let me kiss my children goodbye. I could have harmed you, but the God of your father warned me last night. And then a smaller, sharper question: but why did you steal my gods?
Jacob's answer to the first charge: I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force. To the second: search for the gods — whoever has them shall not live. He doesn't know Rachel has them.
Jacob's answer to the first charge: I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force. To the second: search for the gods — whoever has them shall not live. He doesn't know Rachel has them.

Laban searches every tent. He comes to Rachel's. Rachel has hidden the teraphim in her camel's saddle and is sitting on them. Forgive me for not rising — the custom of women is on me. The household idols are sat upon by a menstruating woman. The reduction is total.

Laban searches every tent. He comes to Rachel's. Rachel has hidden the teraphim in her camel's saddle and is sitting on them. Forgive me for not rising — the custom of women is on me. The household idols are sat upon by a menstruating woman. The reduction is total.

Laban searches every tent. He comes to Rachel's. Rachel has hidden the teraphim in her camel's saddle and is sitting on them. Forgive me for not rising — the custom of women is on me. The household idols are sat upon by a menstruating woman. The reduction is total.
Jacob's righteous anger now erupts. What is my offense? What is my sin? You have hunted me down. You searched every belonging — and found nothing. Twenty years of suppressed grievance comes out.
Jacob's righteous anger now erupts. What is my offense? What is my sin? You have hunted me down. You searched every belonging — and found nothing. Twenty years of suppressed grievance comes out.

Twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and goats never miscarried. I never ate one of your rams. When animals were torn, I bore the loss. The day's heat ate me; the night's frost too. Sleep fled from my eyes.

Twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and goats never miscarried. I never ate one of your rams. When animals were torn, I bore the loss. The day's heat ate me; the night's frost too. Sleep fled from my eyes.

Twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and goats never miscarried. I never ate one of your rams. When animals were torn, I bore the loss. The day's heat ate me; the night's frost too. Sleep fled from my eyes.

Twenty years — fourteen for your daughters, six for the cattle — and you changed my wages ten times. If the God of my father, the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, you would have sent me away empty. God has seen my affliction and rebuked thee last night.

Twenty years — fourteen for your daughters, six for the cattle — and you changed my wages ten times. If the God of my father, the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, you would have sent me away empty. God has seen my affliction and rebuked thee last night.
Laban steps back. These daughters are my daughters; these children are my children; this flock is my flock. What can I do for them now? Come, let us make a covenant between us — for me and for you.
Laban steps back. These daughters are my daughters; these children are my children; this flock is my flock. What can I do for them now? Come, let us make a covenant between us — for me and for you.

Jacob sets up a pillar and calls his men to gather stones. They build a heap. They eat there together. Laban names it Jegar-sahadutha in his Aramaic; Jacob names it Galeed in his Hebrew. Both names mean the same thing: heap of witness.

Jacob sets up a pillar and calls his men to gather stones. They build a heap. They eat there together. Laban names it Jegar-sahadutha in his Aramaic; Jacob names it Galeed in his Hebrew. Both names mean the same thing: heap of witness.

Jacob sets up a pillar and calls his men to gather stones. They build a heap. They eat there together. Laban names it Jegar-sahadutha in his Aramaic; Jacob names it Galeed in his Hebrew. Both names mean the same thing: heap of witness.

This heap is witness between us, says Laban. Therefore the place is called Galeed, and also Mizpah — the LORD watch between you and me, when we are absent one from another. The famous Mizpah blessing is born in suspicion, not affection.

This heap is witness between us, says Laban. Therefore the place is called Galeed, and also Mizpah — the LORD watch between you and me, when we are absent one from another. The famous Mizpah blessing is born in suspicion, not affection.

If you afflict my daughters, or take other wives, God is witness. This pillar I have set up — let the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us. Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac. The covenant is wary, but sealed.

If you afflict my daughters, or take other wives, God is witness. This pillar I have set up — let the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us. Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac. The covenant is wary, but sealed.

If you afflict my daughters, or take other wives, God is witness. This pillar I have set up — let the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us. Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac. The covenant is wary, but sealed.

If you afflict my daughters, or take other wives, God is witness. This pillar I have set up — let the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us. Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac. The covenant is wary, but sealed.

Jacob offers a sacrifice on the mount, calls his men to a meal, and they spend the night. Early in the morning Laban kisses his daughters and grandchildren goodbye, blesses them, and goes home. The grandfather and the son-in-law will never meet again.

Jacob offers a sacrifice on the mount, calls his men to a meal, and they spend the night. Early in the morning Laban kisses his daughters and grandchildren goodbye, blesses them, and goes home. The grandfather and the son-in-law will never meet again.
